Compromise, Optimization and Commons schools narrow the ability to address problems conceived of 
through a Type 4 lens to those that are synergistic with Types 3, 2 and 1 conceptions (Cashore and

was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises 
permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This 
As of September 12, 2025 2:27 pm 
 
- 28 -
means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the 
prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this 
approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be 
used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be 
difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this 
approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data 
that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data 
even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced 
approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques 
that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time.   
 
 
Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two 
distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a 
“branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask  “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 
4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding 
for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was 
more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually 
pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify

The results of a set theoretic analysis for outcomes ~O1 and ~O2.15 The 11 core conﬁgurations (I–XI) are the logically
reduced empirical observations of conditions that are sufﬁcient to cause the outcome under scrutiny. Large full circles (●)
indicate core causal conditions that must be present to cause the outcome; large crossed out circles () indicate core causal
conditions that must be absent; small full circles (●) indicate contributing causal conditions that must be present to cause the
outcome; and small crossed out circles (ø) indicate contributing causal conditions that must be absent. Fiss (2011) provides a
highly accessible discussion of this notation.
© 2018 The Authors. Regulation & Governance Published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd

factuals used were based on the assumptions identiﬁed in the literature
discussed earlier in the article, resulting in the intermediary solution (Fiss 2011). It should be noted that, while the
intermediary solution may look “simpler” than the complex solution, it provides less categorical delineation.
5. Discussion: Further exploration of the diffusion network perspective
The raw data (Table 2) provide empirical evidence for a recurring assumption in the literature on voluntary pro-
grams. Voluntary programs that indicate good performance in terms of attracting participants by no means
always also indicate good performance in changing the participants’ behavior, and vice versa. This indicates that
various metrics are required to assess the overall performance of a voluntary program – that is, the extent to
which it helps to address the societal problem it targets (Borck & Coglianese 2009; Potoski & Prakash 2009; van
der Heijden 2012). The iterative steps of data–theory matching provide evidence for other core assumptions in
the literature on voluntary program performance. That is, various conditions interact in how they affect program
performance (conjunctural causation), and different theoretical models can explain program performance (equi-
ﬁnality) (Fowler & Rauch 2006; Thøgersen 2010; Boyd & Ghosh 2013).

e outcome data for outcome O1 (negative),
but show opposite observations for the stringency of enforcement (present in Case 1; absent in Case 2). It is not
surprising that none of these very simplistic theoretical models explain any of the observed variances in the out-
come data. After all, the literature review pointed out that the outcomes of voluntary programs are likely to be
caused by different interacting conditions (i.e. conjunctural causation).
In step 2 of the iterative process, the full set of conditions from the club theory perspective (Potoski &
Prakash 2009) were explored – that is PR * PE * R1 * R2 * R3 (here “*” indicates the logical AND). While the
club theory perspective captures a considerable part of the variance observed in the second outcome
(0.94 * 19/22 + 0.67 * 3/22 = 90%), its explanatory reach is too limited to capture the variance observed in the
ﬁrst outcome. For O1 there are data conﬂicts like those observed for the individual conditions in step 1.12
In step 3, theoretical models made up of pairs of conditions were then explored, in which the full club theory
was treated as a condition. In this step, two theoretical models capture a part or even the full variance in the out-
come observations. That is, the model that combines club theory with government involvement in the voluntary
